comments:
Summary
This paper addresses the practical problem of reducing the cost of preference elicitation by using a case-based approach: when a new user provides partial preference information, the system finds similar complete (or partially specified) preference structures from a population of previously elicited users and uses the closest as a default. The paper studies three distance measures between preference structures — Spearman's footrule, Euclidean distance (on representative utilities), and a new "probabilistic distance" defined as the probability that two users disagree on the ranking of a randomly chosen pair — and extends these concepts to partially specified preference orders. Computational issues (complexity of counting/generating linear extensions, use of Markov chain Monte Carlo to sample linear extensions) are addressed. The authors present MovieFinder, an experimental recommender demonstrating the approach.

Strengths
- The paper tackles a relevant and practical problem (making decision-theoretic preference models usable in interactive systems) and connects decision theory, order theory, and case-based/recommender ideas.
- The probabilistic distance is a neat, well-motivated notion that naturally extends to weak orders and avoids arbitrary numeric scaling of utilities or ranks.
- The paper is careful to discuss computational complexity (showing the #P-hardness of exact counting) and proposes a viable approximation approach using existing MCMC methods (Bubley & Dyer chain) and Monte Carlo estimation for distances among partial orders.
- The paper contains both theoretical results (metric properties, treatment of partial orders) and an implemented prototype (MovieFinder), showing feasibility.

Weaknesses and suggestions
- Empirical evaluation is limited. The MovieFinder experiment (10 students, 50 movies) is useful as a proof of concept but too small to show robustness, to compare the different distance measures in practice, or to validate the performance of the MCMC sampling in realistic settings. A larger user data set, quantitative retrieval/ recommendation metrics, and comparisons (e.g., with standard collaborative filtering similarity measures) would strengthen the claims.
- The paper leaves open the choice among the distance measures. While the theoretical differences are described (relative equivalence, different behaviors), guidance on when to prefer the probabilistic distance versus Euclidean/Spearman measures (e.g., sensitivity to utility scaling, robustness to noise, empirical discriminative power) would be valuable.
- The continuous (uncertainty) case introduces the probabilistic distance defined by integrating over the simplex of prospects. The paper sketches this and gives examples, but operational details are sparse: how to sample prospects (or approximate integrals) in practice, numerical stability, and runtime are not fully developed.
- Computational cost: while the use of MCMC to sample linear extensions is appropriate, the practical running time for larger numbers of outcomes (n) is only sketched. More concrete runtime measurements and guidelines about feasible problem sizes would help practitioners.
- The paper would benefit from a clearer discussion of active elicitation strategies: i.e., how to choose the next elicitation question to reduce ambiguity among candidate matches. The authors note the importance of question order (and give the genre attribute example) but do not provide a formal active selection method.
- Some proofs and arguments are presented at a high level; more detailed proofs (or pointers to the exact lemmas used from the literature) would increase confidence in some claims (e.g., the zero-volume argument for probabilistic distance identifying strategic equivalence).

Minor issues
- A few typographical/formatting issues and some sentences with minor grammar problems (easily corrected).
- The related work discussion could be expanded to more thoroughly compare to collaborative filtering and to recent case-based or preference elicitation literature (though the paper does cite key works available at the time).

Overall assessment
This is a well-motivated and solid contribution that combines theoretical definitions, complexity analysis, and a prototype implementation. The probabilistic distance is an appealing addition to the set of similarity measures for preference structures because it avoids arbitrary scaling choices and naturally handles weak orders. The treatment of partially specified orders and the practical use of sampling/MCMC are appropriate and important. To raise the practical impact, the paper should include more extensive empirical validation, clearer operational details for the uncertainty case, and additional development of active elicitation and prototype selection (cluster representatives) strategies. The paper will be of interest to researchers working on preference elicitation, recommender systems, and decision-theoretic user modeling.
